Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{jalink|Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376}}{{jalink|Tsushima City|Q991032}}{{jalink|Izuhara Town|Q11410123}} Tsutsu |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Takuzudama-no-Kami|Q133097744}}?, {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} |Founded = Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = |Other names = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki|label=Takuzudama
Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Entrance of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii gate]] '''Takuzudama Shrine''' (たくずだまじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Tsutsu, {{jalink|Izuhara Town|Q11410123}}, {{jalink|Tsushima City|Q991032}}, {{jalink|Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376}}. === Enshrined deities === {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} === History === There is a tradition that the shrine was founded during the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, but this is considered a later attribution, and the precise date and circumstances of its establishment are unknown. In ancient times it was called “Takuzuda Shrine,” without the character for “tama” (soul). The “Takuzuda Shrine” listed among the thirteen shrines of Shimogata District, Tsushima Province, in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} is identified with this shrine. Although the enshrined deities are said to be five deities including Amaterasu Ōmikami, the original deity was {{jalink|Takuzuda-no-Kami}}, a god unique to {{jalink|Tsushima|Q52051}}. In the entry for February 5, Jōwa 4 (837) in the 『{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}』, there is a record of Takuzuto-no-Kami being promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, which is thought to refer to the deity of this shrine. The shrine was part of Tsushima’s distinctive syncretic faith known as Tendō worship, which blended Shinto and Buddhism, and functioned together with a Kannon hall, where rituals were conducted by temple priests{{jalink|Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004. Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{jalink|Goryeo|Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{jalink|waniguchi|Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{jalink|King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{jalink|Jinju|Q42144|Jinju City}}, {{jalink|South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151}}, {{jalink|Republic of Korea|Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{jalink|Tripitaka|Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{jalink|List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{jalink|Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{jalink|Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|Kannon-ji}} (the {{jalink|Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{jalink|Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{jalink|Koreans|Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{jalink|Nikkei|Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{jalink|Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{jalink|Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829}} * {{jalink|Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099}} * {{jalink|Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602}} * {{jalink|Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]

== * https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183378 {{commons category|Category:Takuzudama-jinja}} [[JA:多久頭魂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430613}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Takuzudama Shrine |Image = [[File:Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|250px|Takuzudama Shrine]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{jalink|Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376}}{{jalink|Tsushima City|Q991032}}{{jalink|Izuhara Town|Q11410123}} Tsutsu |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Takuzudama-no-Kami|Q133097744}}?, {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} |Founded = Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = |Other names = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki|label=Takuzudama
Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Entrance of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii gate]] '''Takuzudama Shrine''' (たくずだまじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Tsutsu, {{jalink|Izuhara Town|Q11410123}}, {{jalink|Tsushima City|Q991032}}, {{jalink|Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376}}. === Enshrined deities === {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} === History === There is a tradition that the shrine was founded during the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, but this is considered a later attribution, and the precise date and circumstances of its establishment are unknown. In ancient times it was called “Takuzuda Shrine,” without the character for “tama” (soul). The “Takuzuda Shrine” listed among the thirteen shrines of Shimogata District, Tsushima Province, in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} is identified with this shrine. Although the enshrined deities are said to be five deities including Amaterasu Ōmikami, the original deity was {{jalink|Takuzuda-no-Kami}}, a god unique to {{jalink|Tsushima|Q52051}}. In the entry for February 5, Jōwa 4 (837) in the 『{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}』, there is a record of Takuzuto-no-Kami being promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, which is thought to refer to the deity of this shrine. The shrine was part of Tsushima’s distinctive syncretic faith known as Tendō worship, which blended Shinto and Buddhism, and functioned together with a Kannon hall, where rituals were conducted by temple priests{{jalink|Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004. Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{jalink|Goryeo|Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{jalink|waniguchi|Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{jalink|King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{jalink|Jinju|Q42144|Jinju City}}, {{jalink|South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151}}, {{jalink|Republic of Korea|Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{jalink|Tripitaka|Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{jalink|List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{jalink|Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{jalink|Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|Kannon-ji}} (the {{jalink|Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{jalink|Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{jalink|Koreans|Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{jalink|Nikkei|Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{jalink|Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{jalink|Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829}} * {{jalink|Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099}} * {{jalink|Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602}} * {{jalink|Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]

== * https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183378 {{commons category|Category:Takuzudama-jinja}} [[JA:多久頭魂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430613}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Takuzudama Shrine |Image = [[File:Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|250px|Takuzudama Shrine]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{jalink|Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376}}{{jalink|Tsushima City|Q991032}}{{jalink|Izuhara Town|Q11410123}} Tsutsu |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Takuzudama-no-Kami|Q133097744}}?, {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} |Founded = Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = |Other names = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki|label=Takuzudama
Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Entrance of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii gate]] '''Takuzudama Shrine''' (たくずだまじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Tsutsu, {{jalink|Izuhara Town|Q11410123}}, {{jalink|Tsushima City|Q991032}}, {{jalink|Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376}}. === Enshrined deities === {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} === History === There is a tradition that the shrine was founded during the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, but this is considered a later attribution, and the precise date and circumstances of its establishment are unknown. In ancient times it was called “Takuzuda Shrine,” without the character for “tama” (soul). The “Takuzuda Shrine” listed among the thirteen shrines of Shimogata District, Tsushima Province, in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} is identified with this shrine. Although the enshrined deities are said to be five deities including Amaterasu Ōmikami, the original deity was {{jalink|Takuzuda-no-Kami}}, a god unique to {{jalink|Tsushima|Q52051}}. In the entry for February 5, Jōwa 4 (837) in the 『{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}』, there is a record of Takuzuto-no-Kami being promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, which is thought to refer to the deity of this shrine. The shrine was part of Tsushima’s distinctive syncretic faith known as Tendō worship, which blended Shinto and Buddhism, and functioned together with a Kannon hall, where rituals were conducted by temple priests{{jalink|Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004. Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{jalink|Goryeo|Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{jalink|waniguchi|Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{jalink|King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{jalink|Jinju|Q42144|Jinju City}}, {{jalink|South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151}}, {{jalink|Republic of Korea|Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{jalink|Tripitaka|Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{jalink|List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{jalink|Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{jalink|Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|Kannon-ji}} (the {{jalink|Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{jalink|Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{jalink|Koreans|Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{jalink|Nikkei|Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{jalink|Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{jalink|Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829}} * {{jalink|Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099}} * {{jalink|Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602}} * {{jalink|Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]

== * https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183378 {{commons category|Category:Takuzudama-jinja}} [[JA:多久頭魂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430613}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Takuzudama Shrine |Image = [[File:Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|250px|Takuzudama Shrine]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{jalink|Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376}}{{jalink|Tsushima City|Q991032}}{{jalink|Izuhara Town|Q11410123}} Tsutsu |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Takuzudama-no-Kami|Q133097744}}?, {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} |Founded = Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}} |Main hall = |Other names = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki|label=Takuzudama
Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Entrance of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii gate]] '''Takuzudama Shrine''' (たくずだまじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Tsutsu, {{jalink|Izuhara Town|Q11410123}}, {{jalink|Tsushima City|Q991032}}, {{jalink|Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376}}. === Enshrined deities === {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} === History === There is a tradition that the shrine was founded during the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}, but this is considered a later attribution, and the precise date and circumstances of its establishment are unknown. In ancient times it was called “Takuzuda Shrine,” without the character for “tama” (soul). The “Takuzuda Shrine” listed among the thirteen shrines of Shimogata District, Tsushima Province, in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} is identified with this shrine. Although the enshrined deities are said to be five deities including Amaterasu Ōmikami, the original deity was {{jalink|Takuzuda-no-Kami}}, a god unique to {{jalink|Tsushima|Q52051}}. In the entry for February 5, Jōwa 4 (837) in the 『{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}』, there is a record of Takuzuto-no-Kami being promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, which is thought to refer to the deity of this shrine. The shrine was part of Tsushima’s distinctive syncretic faith known as Tendō worship, which blended Shinto and Buddhism, and functioned together with a Kannon hall, where rituals were conducted by temple priests{{jalink|Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004. Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{jalink|Goryeo|Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{jalink|waniguchi|Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{jalink|King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{jalink|Jinju|Q42144|Jinju City}}, {{jalink|South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151}}, {{jalink|Republic of Korea|Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{jalink|Tripitaka|Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{jalink|List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{jalink|Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{jalink|Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|Kannon-ji}} (the {{jalink|Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{jalink|Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{jalink|Koreans|Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{jalink|Nikkei|Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{jalink|Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{jalink|Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829}} * {{jalink|Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099}} * {{jalink|Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602}} * {{jalink|Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]

Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Entrance of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii gate]] '''Takuzudama Shrine''' (たくずだまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Tsutsu, {{ill|lt=Izuhara Town|Q11410123|WD=Q11410123}}, {{ill|lt=Tsushima City|Q991032|WD=Q991032}}, {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376|WD=Q169376}}. === Enshrined deities === {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} === History === There is a tradition that the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, but this is considered a later attribution, and the precise date and circumstances of its establishment are unknown. In ancient times it was called “Takuzuda Shrine,” without the character for “tama” (soul). The “Takuzuda Shrine” listed among the thirteen shrines of Shimogata District, Tsushima Province, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} is identified with this shrine. Although the enshrined deities are said to be five deities including Amaterasu Ōmikami, the original deity was {{ill||lt=Takuzuda-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}, a god unique to {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051}}. In the entry for February 5, Jōwa 4 (837) in the 『{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}}』, there is a record of Takuzuto-no-Kami being promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, which is thought to refer to the deity of this shrine. The shrine was part of Tsushima’s distinctive syncretic faith known as Tendō worship, which blended Shinto and Buddhism, and functioned together with a Kannon hall, where rituals were conducted by temple priests{{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004. Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{ill|lt=Goryeo|Q28208|WD=Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{ill|lt=waniguchi|Q11674315|WD=Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{ill|lt=King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|WD=Q368892|lt=Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{ill|lt=Jinju|Q42144|WD=Q42144|lt=Jinju City}}, {{ill|lt=South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151|WD=Q41151}}, {{ill|lt=Republic of Korea|Q884|WD=Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{ill|lt=Tripitaka|Q82800|WD=Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{ill|lt=List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|WD=Q11652661|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602|lt=Kannon-ji}} (the {{ill|lt=Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378|WD=Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567|WD=Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{ill|lt=Koreans|Q11271251|WD=Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{ill|lt=Nikkei|Q1077694|WD=Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829|WD=Q11517829}} * {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} * {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602}} * {{ill|lt=Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450|WD=Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]

Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{ill|lt=Goryeo|Q28208|WD=Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{ill|lt=waniguchi|Q11674315|WD=Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{ill|lt=King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|WD=Q368892|lt=Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{ill|lt=Jinju|Q42144|WD=Q42144|lt=Jinju City}}, {{ill|lt=South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151|WD=Q41151}}, {{ill|lt=Republic of Korea|Q884|WD=Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{ill|lt=Tripitaka|Q82800|WD=Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{ill|lt=List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|WD=Q11652661|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602|lt=Kannon-ji}} (the {{ill|lt=Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378|WD=Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567|WD=Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{ill|lt=Koreans|Q11271251|WD=Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{ill|lt=Nikkei|Q1077694|WD=Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829|WD=Q11517829}} * {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} * {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602}} * {{ill|lt=Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450|WD=Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]

Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Entrance of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii gate]] '''Takuzudama Shrine''' (たくずだまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Tsutsu, {{ill|lt=Izuhara Town|Q11410123|WD=Q11410123}}, {{ill|lt=Tsushima City|Q991032|WD=Q991032}}, {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376|WD=Q169376}}. === Enshrined deities === {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} === History === There is a tradition that the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, but this is considered a later attribution, and the precise date and circumstances of its establishment are unknown. In ancient times it was called “Takuzuda Shrine,” without the character for “tama” (soul). The “Takuzuda Shrine” listed among the thirteen shrines of Shimogata District, Tsushima Province, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} is identified with this shrine. Although the enshrined deities are said to be five deities including Amaterasu Ōmikami, the original deity was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takuzuda-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, a god unique to {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051}}. In the entry for February 5, Jōwa 4 (837) in the 『{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}}』, there is a record of Takuzuto-no-Kami being promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, which is thought to refer to the deity of this shrine. The shrine was part of Tsushima’s distinctive syncretic faith known as Tendō worship, which blended Shinto and Buddhism, and functioned together with a Kannon hall, where rituals were conducted by temple priests{{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004. Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{ill|lt=Goryeo|Q28208|WD=Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{ill|lt=waniguchi|Q11674315|WD=Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{ill|lt=King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|WD=Q368892|lt=Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{ill|lt=Jinju|Q42144|WD=Q42144|lt=Jinju City}}, {{ill|lt=South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151|WD=Q41151}}, {{ill|lt=Republic of Korea|Q884|WD=Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{ill|lt=Tripitaka|Q82800|WD=Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{ill|lt=List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|WD=Q11652661|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602|lt=Kannon-ji}} (the {{ill|lt=Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378|WD=Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567|WD=Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{ill|lt=Koreans|Q11271251|WD=Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{ill|lt=Nikkei|Q1077694|WD=Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829|WD=Q11517829}} * {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} * {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602}} * {{ill|lt=Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450|WD=Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]

Shrine |Image = [[File:Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|250px|Takuzudama Shrine]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376|WD=Q169376}}{{ill|lt=Tsushima City|Q991032|WD=Q991032}}{{ill|lt=Izuhara Town|Q11410123|WD=Q11410123}} Tsutsu |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Takuzudama-no-Kami|Q133097744|WD=Q133097744}}?, {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} |Founded = Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}} |Main hall = |Other names = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki|label=Takuzudama
Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Entrance of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii gate]] '''Takuzudama Shrine''' (たくずだまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Tsutsu, {{ill|lt=Izuhara Town|Q11410123|WD=Q11410123}}, {{ill|lt=Tsushima City|Q991032|WD=Q991032}}, {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376|WD=Q169376}}. === Enshrined deities === {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} === History === There is a tradition that the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, but this is considered a later attribution, and the precise date and circumstances of its establishment are unknown. In ancient times it was called “Takuzuda Shrine,” without the character for “tama” (soul). The “Takuzuda Shrine” listed among the thirteen shrines of Shimogata District, Tsushima Province, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} is identified with this shrine. Although the enshrined deities are said to be five deities including Amaterasu Ōmikami, the original deity was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takuzuda-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, a god unique to {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051}}. In the entry for February 5, Jōwa 4 (837) in the 『{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}}』, there is a record of Takuzuto-no-Kami being promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, which is thought to refer to the deity of this shrine. The shrine was part of Tsushima’s distinctive syncretic faith known as Tendō worship, which blended Shinto and Buddhism, and functioned together with a Kannon hall, where rituals were conducted by temple priests{{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004. Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{ill|lt=Goryeo|Q28208|WD=Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{ill|lt=waniguchi|Q11674315|WD=Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{ill|lt=King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|WD=Q368892|lt=Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{ill|lt=Jinju|Q42144|WD=Q42144|lt=Jinju City}}, {{ill|lt=South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151|WD=Q41151}}, {{ill|lt=Republic of Korea|Q884|WD=Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{ill|lt=Tripitaka|Q82800|WD=Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{ill|lt=List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|WD=Q11652661|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602|lt=Kannon-ji}} (the {{ill|lt=Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378|WD=Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567|WD=Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{ill|lt=Koreans|Q11271251|WD=Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{ill|lt=Nikkei|Q1077694|WD=Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829|WD=Q11517829}} * {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} * {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602}} * {{ill|lt=Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450|WD=Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]

== * https://jmapps.ne.jp/kokugakuin/det.html?data_id=183378 {{commons category|Category:Takuzudama-jinja}} [[JA:多久頭魂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430613}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Takuzudama Shrine |Image = [[File:Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|250px|Takuzudama Shrine]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376|WD=Q169376}}{{ill|lt=Tsushima City|Q991032|WD=Q991032}}{{ill|lt=Izuhara Town|Q11410123|WD=Q11410123}} Tsutsu |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Takuzudama-no-Kami|Q133097744|WD=Q133097744}}?, {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} |Founded = Traditionally attributed to the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}} |Main hall = |Other names = |Pilgrimage designation = |Annual festival = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki|label=Takuzudama
Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Entrance of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Torii gate]] '''Takuzudama Shrine''' (たくずだまじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Tsutsu, {{ill|lt=Izuhara Town|Q11410123|WD=Q11410123}}, {{ill|lt=Tsushima City|Q991032|WD=Q991032}}, {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376|WD=Q169376}}. === Enshrined deities === {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Hikohononinigi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto}} === History === There is a tradition that the shrine was founded during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}, but this is considered a later attribution, and the precise date and circumstances of its establishment are unknown. In ancient times it was called “Takuzuda Shrine,” without the character for “tama” (soul). The “Takuzuda Shrine” listed among the thirteen shrines of Shimogata District, Tsushima Province, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} is identified with this shrine. Although the enshrined deities are said to be five deities including Amaterasu Ōmikami, the original deity was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takuzuda-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, a god unique to {{ill|lt=Tsushima|Q52051|WD=Q52051}}. In the entry for February 5, Jōwa 4 (837) in the 『{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}}』, there is a record of Takuzuto-no-Kami being promoted from no rank to Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, which is thought to refer to the deity of this shrine. The shrine was part of Tsushima’s distinctive syncretic faith known as Tendō worship, which blended Shinto and Buddhism, and functioned together with a Kannon hall, where rituals were conducted by temple priests{{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004. Tendō worship is a syncretism of mother-and-child deity worship, sun worship, mountain worship, and other beliefs. Originally, the shrine had no sanctuary building and instead served as a place of distant worship of the sacred, restricted mountain Tendōyama (Tatera-yama). After the {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}} in the Meiji period, the Kannon hall of Tsutsutera, which had been a shrine temple, was used as the shrine building; however, this Kannon hall was destroyed by fire in 1956*Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, pp. 1011–1012, and *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, p. 595. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Properties (national designation) === * **Bronze drum (Kinko)** — From the {{ill|lt=Goryeo|Q28208|WD=Q28208}} period, made of bronze, diameter 81.2 cm. “Kinko” refers to what is known in Japan as a {{ill|lt=waniguchi|Q11674315|WD=Q11674315}}. It bears an inscription including “Zen-gen □ Mi fifth month day, Jinyang Prefecture,” as well as an additional inscription dated Shōhei 12 (1357). “Zen-gen” is thought to be a private era name, and “□ Mi” is considered to correspond to the yimi year (1245), the 32nd year of the reign of {{ill|lt=King Gojong of Goryeo|Q368892|WD=Q368892|lt=Gojong}}. It was produced in the same year in what is now {{ill|lt=Jinju|Q42144|WD=Q42144|lt=Jinju City}}, {{ill|lt=South Gyeongsang Province|Q41151|WD=Q41151}}, {{ill|lt=Republic of Korea|Q884|WD=Q884}}*Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, pp. 166–167. [[File:Bell of Takuzudama Shrine.jpg|thumb|230px|Temple bell]] * **Temple bell** — A Japanese-style bell cast in Kōei 3 (1344) by metalworkers from Matsuura in Hizen Province. A long inscription appears in the pond-shaped section above the striking point, stating that the original bell was first made in Kankō 5 (1008) and was recast in Kōei 3 (1344). The craftsmen are identified as the master carpenter Kakuen and the assistant craftsman Suenaka from Yamashita Manor in Kamimatsuura, Hizen Province. * **Goryeo-printed Tripitaka** — 3 volumes, 2 fascicles, 1,016 books (with appended *Great Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra* [Japanese edition 318 fascicles, manuscript 6 fascicles], total 324 fascicles), designated in fiscal year 2017Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117, September 15, 2017. === Theft incident === The “{{ill|lt=Tripitaka|Q82800|WD=Q82800}}” that had been in the shrine’s possession was designated as a {{ill|lt=List of Nagasaki Prefecture designated cultural properties|Q11652661|WD=Q11652661|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture cultural property}}[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/bunkazai_details_nomap.php?id=260 “Tripitaka of Takuzudama Shrine”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} Nagasaki Prefecture, Cultural Properties of Nagasaki Prefecture, but it was stolen in October 2012 along with the “Standing Bronze Buddha Tathāgata” from {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} and the “Seated Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva” from {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602|lt=Kannon-ji}} (the {{ill|lt=Tsushima Buddhist statue theft incident|Q18337378|WD=Q18337378}})[http://www.nagasaki-np.co.jp/kiji/20121010/02.shtml “Two additional cases of cultural property theft discovered”]{{dead link|date=January 2018}} {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Shimbun|Q11652567|WD=Q11652567}}, October 10, 2012. In January 2013, a group of {{ill|lt=Koreans|Q11271251|WD=Q11271251}} who attempted to bring the items into South Korea to sell them were detained, and the two Buddhist statues were recovered; however, the perpetrators stated that the Tripitaka had been “discarded in the fields and mountains around the shrine”[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG3000V_Q3A130C1CR0000/ “Man detained in Tsushima Buddhist statue theft; South Korean police recover stolen items”] {{ill|lt=Nikkei|Q1077694|WD=Q1077694}}, January 30, 2013. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nagasaki Prefecture Place Names*, Heibonsha, 2001 * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten: Nagasaki Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten, 1987 * *Illustrated Guide to Newly Designated Important Cultural Properties 4: Crafts I*, 1981 * {{ill|lt=Masataka Suzuki|Q11648975|WD=Q11648975}} “Rituals and Village Space in Tsutsu, Tsushima,” in *Cosmology of Ritual and Space—Tsushima and Okinawa*, Shunjūsha, 2004 * *Tsushima Shrine Gazetteer*, Tsushima Shrine Representatives Association of Nagasaki Prefecture, 1966. National Diet Library Digital Collection https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3451609 (accessed 2025-06-09) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Issue of the return of cultural properties taken out of the Korean Peninsula|Q11517829|WD=Q11517829}} * {{ill|lt=Watatsumi Shrine|Q11559099|WD=Q11559099}} * {{ill|lt=Kannon-ji (Tsushima City)|Q11630602|WD=Q11630602}} * {{ill|lt=Komoda Hama Shrine|Q17991450|WD=Q17991450}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takuzudama Shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagasaki Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Takuzudama Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tsushima]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:対馬市の歴史]] [[Category:対馬島の式内社]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下県郡]] [[Category:長崎県の重要文化財]] [[Category:文化財盗難被害に遭った寺社]] [[Category:対馬市の建築物]]
